meta.title: onym

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: onym
root.mentor_note: onym is the root behind nearly every word English has for kinds of names.

attempt.direction_prompt: The author’s real name, or a made up pen name?

context_sentences: The novel was published under a pseudonym, and its true author was not revealed for a decade.
She writes her crime fiction under a pseudonym so it never gets shelved beside her academic work.
The editor replaced “utilise” with its plainer synonym “use” throughout the entire report.
Ask a room of ten people for a synonym of “happy” and you will get ten slightly different words.
The exam asked students to supply the correct antonym for each of ten adjectives.
He argued that “brave” has no perfect antonym, since “cowardly” carries a judgment “brave” does not.

construct_options.text: A word that means the opposite of another word
A word borrowed directly from a foreign language
A word that means nearly the same as another word

context_sentences: The riddle depended entirely on a homonym: the word for a tree branch and the word for a bank’s branch.
Young readers often stumble on homonyms like “bark,” unsure whether it means a sound or a tree’s covering.

construct_options.text: A word that sounds or is spelled the same as another but differs in meaning
A name passed down within the same family for generations
A word invented for a single specific occasion
